description: Comprehensive safety requirements by task type for instrumentation construction, commissioning, and maintenance

jsa_items: Verify line depressurized and drained before opening connections
Use bleed valve to confirm zero energy
Install root valves first, close before connecting transmitter
Never stand in front of open process connection — fluid under pressure can spray
Use thread sealant compatible with process fluid
Never use Teflon tape on oxygen service — fire/explosion risk
Verify instrument rated for area classification before installation
hazards: Stored energy in process lines — even depressurized lines can have pockets
Hot/cold surfaces — burns or frostbite from uninsulated piping
Toxic/flammable process fluids — H2S, hydrocarbons, acids
High-pressure blowout from open connections — can cause severe injury
permits: Hot work permit if welding thermowell or process connection
Confined space permit if inside vessels or tanks
Line break permit for opening any process connection
LOTO for electrical isolation when working on powered instruments

ppe: Safety glasses (MANDATORY — tubing spring-back is the most common eye injury in instrumentation)
Cut-resistant gloves when handling freshly cut tubing
Fall protection above 6 ft when routing on pipe racks
jsa_items: Deburr ALL tubing cuts — inside AND outside edge
Blow out tubing with clean dry air/N2 before connecting to instrument
Never use copper tubing for process service — only for instrument air
Pressure test ALL tubing before connecting to instruments
Cap ALL open tubing ends at the end of each shift — prevent contamination
Use proper tube bender — minimum 5x OD bend radius for 316SS
Support tubing per spec — 5 ft horizontal, 8 ft vertical for 1/2-inch 316SS
hazards: Sharp tubing ends — cuts and puncture wounds
Eye hazard from spring-back during bending — tubing stored energy
Compressed air/N2 stored energy during pressure testing
Falls during routing on pipe racks and structural steel
Pinch points when installing tubing clamps and supports

ppe: Hearing protection near pressure letdown valves (noise >85 dB)
Rigging gloves for handling heavy valves
jsa_items: Verify tag number matches P&ID and line number before installation
Check flow direction arrow matches process flow direction
Remove ALL shipping blocks and stops from actuator before testing
Verify actuator air supply pressure matches nameplate
Stroke valve full range BEFORE connecting to process — verify no binding
Verify fail position matches data sheet — incorrect fail position is a safety hazard
Install bypass around valve if required for startup/maintenance
hazards: Heavy equipment — control valves can weigh hundreds to thousands of pounds
Pinch points during rigging and positioning
Stored energy in actuator springs — can snap closed/open unexpectedly
High noise from pressure letdown in operation (>100 dB possible)
Process fluid release if flange gasket fails
permits: Hot work if welding valve connections

safety: Use ONLY intrinsically safe calibrators in classified (hazardous) areas
LOTO before disconnecting wiring in any energized system
Never apply test pressure exceeding instrument rated range — overpressure can rupture diaphragm
Secure calibration gas cylinders upright with chain — falling cylinders are lethal
Verify calibration gas is not toxic before use in open area
When calibrating in-situ: verify instrument is properly isolated from process
Bleed pressure slowly through bleed valve before disconnecting any pressure instrument
documentation: Record as-found readings BEFORE any adjustment
Apply calibration sticker with date, initials, and next due date
Enter calibration data in CMMS tracking system

safety: NOTIFY operations/control room BEFORE any loop test
Verify DCS/PLC is in MANUAL or TEST mode before driving outputs
Place WARNING TAGS on all control valves being tested
Confirm safety interlocks are properly BYPASSED with written authorization
Two-person communication REQUIRED — one at field, one at control room
Verify no personnel are near control valves or final elements before commanding movement
After test: REMOVE all bypasses, verify interlocks back in service, remove warning tags
loop_testing.test_order: Verify power → check signal at field device → verify at marshalling panel → verify in DCS/PLC → drive output → verify actuator responds → verify feedback → verify fail-safe

requirements: ALL instruments must have appropriate area classification rating matching the location
Explosion-proof (XP): heavy cast housing, all cover bolts MUST be installed — even ONE missing violates rating
Intrinsically Safe (IS): energy-limited circuits, IS barriers required in safe area
Non-incendive (NI): for Division 2 only
Seal fittings (EYS/EYD) within 18 inches of enclosure boundary in Division 1
Only approved tools in classified areas — no unapproved phones, cameras, radios, flashlights
Hot work permits REQUIRED for any spark-producing activity
Never open XP enclosures in energized classified areas without gas testing first

classifications.div_2: Hazardous present only under ABNORMAL conditions
classifications.zone_0: Continuous or long periods — worst case
classifications.zone_2: Abnormal conditions only — equivalent to Div 2

equipment_markings.XP: Explosion-proof — Type d. Heavy cast housing contains explosion.
equipment_markings.IS: Intrinsically Safe — Type ia (Div 1), ib (Div 2). Energy too low to ignite.

safety: BLEED pressure before disconnecting ANY fitting
Instrument air must be clean, dry, oil-free — contaminated air damages instruments
Typical supply: 20 PSI for instruments, 60-80 PSI for valve actuators
Install filter/regulators at each air supply takeoff
NEVER use plant air (shop air) for instruments — contains oil and moisture
Stand clear of tubing connections during pressure testing — lines can whip
hazards: Stored energy in air headers and tubing
Whipping lines if connections fail under pressure
Eye injury from blown debris during blowdown
Noise hazard during large-volume blowdowns

safety: IDENTIFY pipe contents from labels or P&ID BEFORE working near any pipe
Maintain safe distance from uninsulated hot pipes — steam pipes exceed 700°F
Never route instrument tubing in direct contact with hot process piping — use standoff clamps
Report ANY leaks immediately — hissing, frost, discoloration, dripping
Be aware of steam traps — discharge causes severe burns
hazards: Burns from steam/hot oil (can exceed 700°F)
Frostbite from cryogenic lines (LNG, liquid nitrogen, ethylene)
Chemical exposure from leaks — H2S is IDLH at 100 ppm
High-pressure release — small leaks can cut through skin (hydraulic injection)

ppe: Chemical-resistant gloves appropriate for process fluid
Safety glasses with splash guard or chemical goggles
Face shield when working with reagents or sample systems
Supplied air respirator for toxic sample streams (H2S, CO, HCN)
jsa_items: Identify ALL chemicals in sample system before opening any connection
Review SDS for all reagents and calibration gases
Verify sample bypass is OPEN and block valves are CLOSED before working on analyzer
Never smell a sample stream — use detection equipment
Calibration gases may be toxic — use in ventilated area with proper respiratory protection
Liquid reagents: handle with chemical gloves and eye protection
Dispose of waste reagents per site environmental procedures
hazards: Toxic process fluids in sample lines (H2S, HCN, benzene, HF)
Corrosive reagents (acids, caustics)
High-pressure sample streams
Hot sample streams — sample conditioning may not cool fluid completely
Toxic calibration gases

jsa_items: SIS testing requires WRITTEN AUTHORIZATION from operations
Verify process is in safe state before initiating any safety function test
Have spotter at final element before initiating test
NEVER bypass safety function without documented authorization
After test: VERIFY all bypasses removed, all safety functions back in service
Partial stroke tests: verify valve moves and returns without process upset
Full stroke tests may require process shutdown — coordinate with operations
Document ALL test results — SIS testing documentation is regulatory requirement
hazards: Unexpected process upset from safety function activation
Unexpected valve movement — pressure surge, water hammer
Equipment damage from uncontrolled shutdown
Personnel near final elements struck by moving equipment
safety_system_testing.regulatory: SIS testing per SRS defined under IEC 61511 / ISA 84. Missing/late proof tests degrade SIL rating.

jsa_items: Pre-energization checklist COMPLETED and SIGNED before any power applied
Verify ALL construction complete — no open flanges, missing bolts, incomplete welds
Verify instrument air quality meets spec before connecting to instruments
Monitor ALL instruments for abnormal readings during first process introduction
Fire watch present during first hot startup in classified areas
Bump test all gas detectors before relying on them for area monitoring
Communications plan established: positions, radio channels, emergency contacts
Clear STOP WORK authority — anyone can call stop for unsafe conditions
hazards: First-time startup has highest incident rate
Unexpected leaks during initial pressurization
Gas/vapor release during purging and inerting
Instruments may read incorrectly until process stabilizes
Electrical hazards during first energization
commissioning_safety.sequence: Construction complete → Pre-commissioning → Instrument air → Power → Individual instrument checkout → Loop testing → Interlock testing → Process introduction → Performance testing

radiation_safety.applies_to: Nuclear level gauges (Cesium-137 or Cobalt-60 gamma sources)
requirements: Radiation Safety Officer must be present for source installation/removal
Maintain distance from source — follow ALARA principle
Never open source housing without RSO authorization
Annual wipe test required to verify source containment
Dosimeter badge required for personnel working near sources
Source must be SHIELDED when not in measurement service
Report any damage to source housing immediately
radiation_safety.signage: Trefoil radiation symbol required on all source housings and area boundaries

cryogenic_safety.applies_to: Instruments on LNG, liquid nitrogen, liquid oxygen, ethylene, propylene
requirements: Cryogenic-rated gloves — standard work gloves provide NO protection
Face shield when breaking connections — cryogenic spray causes instant frostbite
Materials must be cryogenic-rated — carbon steel shatters at cryogenic temps
316SS or special alloys required for wetted parts
Do NOT use standard gaskets — use PTFE or cryogenic-rated
Oxygen-enriched atmosphere risk when LN2/LOX evaporates — ventilate area
